title: LEPTOSPIROSIS EPIDEMIOLOGY IN INDIA
author: Abdimomunova B.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Leptospirosis interregna’s, a spirochete shaped like a corkscrew, is what causes the infectious disease known as leptospirosis. Leptospirosis is a growing global public health problem that can cause anything from mild infections to deadly pulmonary haemorrhage. The pathogenic Leptospirosis causes a disease that can affect both people and animals. It can spread through contact with infected animals or through water and soil that has been contaminated. Leptospirosis is more common in some places than others because of things like the weather, the growth of cities, and how people raise animals. In humans, it can manifest with severe symptoms, underscoring the necessity of prompt diagnosis. The microscopic agglutination test (MAT) and the enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) are two common tests that are often used to look for leptospirosis infections. Molecular techniques which are greater sensitivity and speed include PCR and qPCR. The disease is more common in people and some animals, such as dogs, buffalo, and rats. This disease is more likely to happen in India coastal areas. In the fight against leptospirosis, there are public health campaigns and surveillance activities. Implementing successful preventative strategies requires an understanding of the epidemiology and prevalence of leptospirosis. Finally, a continued research about public awareness campaigns and addressing research gaps in epidemiology of the disease are critical in mitigating the impact on human and animal health. This review provides important data for public health authorities, veterinarians, scientists and for the public, in general.
